data.title: Primo Brands Initiated at Buy by Truist Securities
Vimian Group AB (publ) terminates liquidity guarantee agreement
data.description: Vimian Group AB (publ) (“Vimian”) today announces that the company has terminated the liquidity guarantee agreement with Carnegie Investment Bank. The purpose of the liquidity guarantee agreement has been to promote liquidity in the company's share, reduce volatility and facilitate trading. Vimian assesses that current trading and liquidity in the share is good and that the benefit of the liquidity guarantee is limited. The agreement expires on 30 April, 2025.

data.title: Flerie publishes merger document
data.description:
THIS ANNOUNCEMENT SHOULD NOT BE DISTRIBUTED, WHETHER DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY, IN OR INTO AUSTRALIA, CANADA, JAPAN, NEW ZEELAND, SOUTH AFRICA OR THE UNITED STATES OF AMERICA. THIS ANNOUNCEMENT SHOULD ALSO NOT BE DISTRIBUTED IN OR INTO ANY OTHER JURISDICTION WHERE SUCH DISTRIBUTION REQUIRES ANY OTHER DOCUMENTATION, REGISTRATION, OR MEASURES BEYOND WHAT IS GOVERNED BY SWEDISH LAW.

On 25 March 2025, Flerie AB (publ) (“Flerie”) announced that the Boards of Directors of Flerie and Toleranzia AB (publ) (“Toleranzia”) jointly have adopted a merger plan to join forces through a merger in which Toleranzia will be absorbed by Flerie (the “Merger”). Following the completion of the Merger, Toleranzia’s operations will be contributed to a new subsidiary of Flerie’s wholly owned subsidiary, Flerie Invest AB. An Annual General Meeting in Flerie to approve the merger plan and the issue of the merger consideration will be held in Flerie on 14 May 2025. An Extraordinary General Meeting in Toleranzia to approve the merger plan will be held on 7 May 2025.

Today, Flerie publishes a merger document regarding the Merger with Toleranzia in accordance with the Stock Market Self-Regulation Committee’s (Sw. Aktiemarknadens självregleringskommittés) Takeover Rules for certain trading platforms. The merger document is published and made available on the respective websites of Flerie and Toleranzia, www.flerie.com and www.toleranzia.se. The merger document has not been reviewed or approved by the Swedish Financial Supervisory Authority (Sw. Finansinspektionen).

Furthermore, the indicative timeline for the Merger is updated to reflect that the approval of the merger plan and the issuance of the merger consideration will take place at Flerie's Annual General Meeting on 14 May 2025. Toleranzia's Extraordinary General Meeting for the approval of the merger plan will be held on 7 May 2025.

Advisors

Flerie has retained Setterwalls Advokatbyrå AB as legal advisor. Toleranzia has engaged Eversheds Sutherland Advokatbyrå AB as legal advisor and has engaged BDO Mälardalen AB to provide a valuation statement (so called fairness opinion) regarding the Merger.

data.title: FAT Brands Enters Partnership to Open 30 Fatburger Locations in France
data.description:
FAT Brands said Friday it has partnered with the group behind Big M CIE to expand Fatburger in France and open 30 locations across the country over the next three years.

The company said five units are set to open in 2026.

Chief Development Officer Taylor Wiederhorn said the company has been seeking opportunities to expand since opening the first Fatburger location in France nearly three years ago.

Shares of FAT Brands fell more than 10% in recent trading.

data.title: 'A Century of Fiction in the New Yorker': The Long and the Short of It
data.description:
By Sam Sacks

Almost as long as there have been stories in the New Yorker there has existed the concept of "the New Yorker story." This is broadly understood to refer to something plotless, nuanced and exquisitely described, a chronicle of East Coast manners that typically features a middle-aged white-collar man bewildered by the complexities of work, family and love. The format is slice-of-life, the concerns staunchly middle-class and the endings muted or elliptical, suggestive of some small, interior transformation. As long as half a century ago, the literary publisher James Laughlin groused that "if you read all [the magazine's] stories every week for a year, you'd begin to think that most of them were written by the same person using different names." In fact, the names themselves compound the sense of homogeneity, since the style's most famous practitioners — O'Hara, Cheever and Updike — were all Johns.

For decades the New Yorker story has been assailed for its provinciality, its rigidity, its blandness. Even the magazine's longtime fiction editor William Maxwell worried about its "cult of clarity and literalness." (In a 1984 parody by Martha Bayles, a chin-stroking story writer changes "floor" to "linoleum floor," and then, on further consideration, to "Congoleum no-wax floor, the kind with the imitation mica.") Yet an admission of the New Yorker's cultural dominance is implicit in the criticism.

In its less sophisticated early years, the magazine's only stories were humor pieces known as "casuals." In the late 1920s, one editor, Katharine White, started to scout for upmarket fare to compete with periodicals such as Esquire and the Saturday Evening Post. But in the 1950s, commercial demand for short fiction evaporated, likely because of the rise of television, and the form began migrating to small literary quarterlies. Among well-paying, mainstream magazines, the New Yorker soon had the field to itself. Now, in the year of its 100th anniversary, its monopoly on the short story in America all but absolute, the magazine has put out a celebratory anthology, "A Century of Fiction in the New Yorker: 1925-2025."

This whoppingly large volume holds 78 works organized in chronological order, from a 1926 comic sketch by E.B. White to Rivka Galchen's "Crown Heights North" from 2024. Deborah Treisman, who has served as the magazine's fiction editor since 2003, has selected the stories from an archive of more than 13,000 pieces of fiction. Her challenge was to produce an array of examples that is representative of the magazine but also compellingly diverse; that illustrates the evolution of styles and values that obtained across the eras; and, withal, that doesn't overlap too much with the New Yorker's many previous collections.

Even the periodical's most skeptical readers would have to say that Ms. Treisman has done a wonderful job, finding a satisfying mix of greatest hits, outstanding B-sides from well-known authors and high-quality surprises. Writers whose names have become bywords, such as J.D. Salinger and Dorothy Parker, share the spotlight with 21st-century house favorites like Tessa Hadley and Yiyun Li.

Crucially included is James Thurber's 1939 classic "The Secret Life of Walter Mitty," which created an archetype for more serious stories that explored the imaginative worlds of ordinary schmoes. One such "insignificant man" receives a startling comeuppance during his evening commute in Cheever's eerie "The Five-Forty-Eight" (1954). In Saul Bellow's "A Father-to-Be" (1955), a young research chemist ponders his coming marriage as he rides the New York subway: "Thoughts very often grow fertile in the subway, because of the motion, the great company, the subtlety of the rider's state as he rattles under streets and rivers, under the foundations of great buildings."

The befuddled everyman persisted beyond his midcentury heyday and soon dispersed across the globe. So we find him drying out in a California rehab facility in Raymond Carver's "Where I'm Calling From" (1982), fretting about women in a New Jersey project in Junot Díaz's "How to Date a Brown Girl (Black Girl, White Girl or Halfie)" (1995) and aimlessly roaming Japan in Haruki Murakami's "U.F.O. in Kushiro" (2001). In Edward P. Jones's "A Rich Man" (2003), a cocky widower in Washington, D.C., is undone by his taste for much younger women, who turn his apartment in a senior-citizen complex into a drug spot. This fine story is as rich, funny and quietly tragic as anything by the Johns.

Though less subject to spoofing, a stock New Yorker female character emerges in this collection, as well. She, too, tends to be passive, domestically restless and faintly risible, like the wife in Mary McCarthy's "The Weeds" (1944), who spends the story ineffectually plotting to escape her manipulative husband. ("She would leave him, she thought, as soon as the petunias had bloomed.") Ann Beattie, a magazine staple, updated this yearning but oddly paralyzed heroine to the mores of the 1970s. When "Cat Person," Kristen Roupenian's story about an awful one-night-stand, went viral in 2017, it marked a full-circle return to acute psychology and subversive sexual candor. As with McCarthy's work, "Cat Person" was confused by many for autobiography.

Ms. Treisman is also keen to pick out writing that runs counter to the reputation for insularity and staidness. Gradually the stories included take on a more international bent. There is a strong lineage of American immigrant writing, running from Isaac Bashevis Singer to Lore Segal to Jhumpa Lahiri. Most eye-catching, perhaps, are the instances when the magazine was willing to break from the house style of domestic realism, opening the door to Shirley Jackson's cause célèbre "The Lottery" (1948), which occasioned more angry letters than any story before it, as well as to the brilliantly surrealist tableaux of Donald Barthelme in the 1960s and Rebecca Curtis in the 2010s.

Even the works that diverge from the norm, though, tend to share a common vision. In the 1998 story "The Telephone Game," the great Irish author William Trevor has a character wonder "why it was that passing incidents seemed more significant in people's lives and their relationships than the enmity or amity of nations." That observation could serve as this anthology's credo. There are exceptions, when politics and world events are confronted directly — Nadine Gordimer's "City Lovers" (1975), for instance, is the powerfully matter-of-fact narrative of an illegal love affair in Apartheid South Africa. But for the most part the stories of "A Century of Fiction in the New Yorker" are invested in the poignancy of the minor incident, the gesture, the inflection. They prize craft, and its mastery, over scope, drama or grand emotion.

In other words, this collection sublimely distills the New Yorker experience: profound pleasure in first-rate execution accompanied by exasperation with the limits of its purview. It's probably evident by now, as the magazine enters its second century, that the chance to complain about the New Yorker's stories is one of the perks of reading them.

Mr. Sacks is the Journal's fiction critic.

data.title: GE Healthcare falls after China begins anti-dumping probe into medical imaging equipment
data.description:
** Shares of medical equipment maker GE Healthcare fall 13.5% to $62.25

** China launches an anti-dumping probe into imports of certain medical X-ray tubes used in CT scans from the U.S.

** China also announces export curbs on some medium and heavy rare-earth metals, including gadolinium, which is used in MRI scans

** China, which contributed ~12% of GEHC's revenue in 2024, is one of the key markets where majority of products sold are imaging equipment

** "The risk is that any ruling from the (Chinese) ministry could impact tender processes and sales within a key market," BTIG analysts say

** "It is clear that the Chinese government is taking steps to prop up a historically lagging medical device industry, and will likely take more proactive measures in the future to favor local Chinese MedTech vs foreign competition" - J.P.Morgan analysts

** An anti-dumping investigation examines whether goods imported into a country are being sold at prices lower than their domestic market prices

** Including session moves, GEHC down ~20% vs ~1% decline in S&P Healthcare Equipment (.SPLRCMED) YTD

data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / April 4, 2025 / If you suffered a loss on your Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. investment and want to learn about a potential recovery under the federal securities laws, follow the link below for more information:

https://zlk.com/pslra-1/intellia-therapeutics-inc-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=141054&wire=1

or contact Joseph E. Levi, Esq. via email at jlevi@levikorsinsky.com or call (212) 363-7500 to speak to our team of experienced shareholder advocates.

<img height="0" src="https://app.accessnewswire.com/api/iframe.ashx?releaseid=1010614&url=https%3a%2f%2fwww.youtube.com%2fembed%2f0o6XOR8lkCk" width="0"/></div></div></div><p><strong>THE LAWSUIT:</strong> A class action securities lawsuit was filed against Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. that seeks to recover losses of shareholders who were adversely affected by alleged securities fraud between July 30, 2024 and January 8, 2025.</p><p><strong>CASE DETAILS:</strong> According to the complaint, defendants provided investors with material information concerning Intellia's Phase 1/2 study evaluating NTLA-3001 for the treatment of alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AATD)-associated lung disease. Defendants' statements included, among other things, confidence in the Company's timeline for the aforementioned study, specifically that Intellia expected to dose the first patient in the second half of 2024. Defendants failed to disclose inter alia that the demand for viral-based editing was rapidly dwindling as non-viral delivery methods became a main target of the scientific research community due to their cost-effectiveness and more efficient development, thus making NTLA-3001 an inefficient program for Intellia to maintain.<br/><br/>The truth emerged on January 9, 2025, when Intellia published a press release announcing Company reorganization. In pertinent part, defendants disclosed that Intellia would be halting all NTLA-3001 research and studies and that the Company would be reducing its workforce by 27% in 2025. Specifically, the Company announced that management decided to focus Intellia's resources on other pharmaceutical development and would be implementing cost saving in the form of a major reduction in force. As a result, defendants pipeline priority readjustment resulted in the Company's once-touted NTLA-3001's discontinuation.</p><p>Following this news, Intellia's stock price fell from a closing market price of $12.02 per share on January 8, 2025 to $10.20 per share on January 10, 2025.</p><p><strong>WHAT'S NEXT?</strong> If you suffered a loss in Intellia stock during the relevant time frame - even if you still hold your shares - go to <a href="https://pr.report/93bg" rel="nofollow">https://zlk.com/pslra-1/intellia-therapeutics-inc-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=141054&wire=1</a> to learn about your rights to seek a recovery. <strong>There is no cost or obligation to participate.</strong></p><p><strong>WHY LEVI & KORSINSKY:</strong> Over the past 20 years, Levi & Korsinsky LLP has established itself as a nationally-recognized securities litigation firm that has secured hundreds of millions of dollars for aggrieved shareholders and built a track record of winning high-stakes cases. THE LAWSUIT: A class action securities lawsuit was filed against Fluence Energy, Inc. that seeks to recover losses of shareholders who were adversely affected by alleged securities fraud between November 29, 2023 and February 10, 2025.

CASE DETAILS: The filed complaint alleges that defendants made false statements and/or concealed that: (1) Fluence's relationship with its founders and largest sources of revenue, Siemens AG and The AES Corporation, was poised to decline; (2) Siemens Energy, Siemens AG's U.S. affiliate, had accused the Company of engineering failures and fraud; (3) Fluence's margins and revenue growth were inflated as Siemens and AES were moving to divest; and (4) based on the foregoing, defendants lacked a reasonable basis for their positive statements related to Fluence's battery energy storage business, as well as related financial results, growth, and prospects.

data.title: JPMorgan Slashes Tesla's Q1 EPS Forecast Amid Delivery Setback
data.description:
April 4 - Tesla had its weakest Q1 in almost three years, delivering only 336,681 vehicles, missing estimates by over 18,000 units. That's a 13% drop year-on-year, and it's the lowest delivery number since Q2 2022.

JPMorgan analysts are saying that CEO Elon Musk's involvement with the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) might be damaging Tesla's brand more than expected. They even warned that the sales dip was worse than they initially thought.

After the disappointing results, Tesla's stock dropped, but it bounced back when a report said President Trump had privately mentioned Musk stepping back from his government role. Tesla quickly denied that, calling it "fake news."

In response, JPMorgan has lowered its Q1 2025 earnings per share forecast to $0.36, down from the previous estimate of $0.40, falling short of the consensus estimate of $0.46.

data.title: Odfjell SE: Annual Report 2024
data.description:
Odfjell SE

Odfjell SE has today published its Annual Report for 2024. The report reflects the company's record-breaking results and performance through a year marked by volatility.  

"Celebrating our company’s 110th anniversary, we reached several new milestones: reporting the strongest financial results in our history, further reducing our fleet’s carbon intensity, investing in people, technology and digitalization, and—most importantly—maintaining our industry-leading safety performance," CEO Harald Fotland said.

The Annual Report and Annual Review include:

  • Financial statements and notes – Odfjell Group and Odfjell SE
  • The Board of Directors’ report including Sustainability statement
  • Business review from Odfjell Tankers, Ship Management and Odfjell Terminals

Read the report and review on Odfjell.com: https://reports.odfjell.com/

ESEF format attached.

IR Contact:

Nils Jørgen Selvik, VP Finance & IR

Tel: +47 920 39 718

E-mail: nils.jorgen.selvik@odfjell.com

The Odfjell Group is one of the leading players in the global market for seaborne transportation and storage of chemicals and other specialty bulk liquids. The Odfjell fleet comprises of approx. 70 ships, which trade both globally and regionally. The tank terminal division consists of four tank terminals and is part of a network of another 10 tank terminals partly owned by related parties. The tank terminals are strategically located at selected international shipping hubs. The Odfjell Group’s headquarters are in Bergen, Norway, and the Group has offices in 12 locations around the world. data.title: Match Group Faces Nominees at Annual Meeting as Activist Anson Funds Goes Public
data.description:
By Denny Jacob

The pressure campaign on Match Group continues.

Activist investor Anson Funds Management said Friday it has nominated Fumbi Chima, Laura Lee and Kelley Morrell for election at the online dating company's annual shareholder meeting.

Anson, one of three known activists with a stake in Match, in a letter to the company's shareholders said while there is a way for Match to grow through a better product, its long-term underperformance was a product of poor corporate governance and board oversight.

"We believe Match's outdated, insular board with deep interlockings is inadequately qualified to oversee a modern technology company primarily serving young adults," said Sagar Gupta, portfolio manager at Anson leading its activism strategy.

Match, which is also dealing with Starboard Value and Elliott Investment Management, didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

Match's stock soared during the pandemic, reaching an all-time high of $175.53 in October 2021. Shares have plummeted since and currently trade closer to $30.

Anson in its letter highlighted connections members of the board have with each other going back to IAC, which Match spun off from around five years ago, and other firms. The activist also pointed to a revolving door of CEOs in recent years, most recently illustrated by the appointment of Spencer Rascoff in February.

"After numerous attempts to constructively engage with the board, we have been left with no choice but to publicly pursue refreshment through replacement," said Anson, but added that it remains open to collaboration in the interest of all shareholders.

Chima's experience includes serving as chief technology officer and chief information officer roles at Adidas AG and Walmart. Lee, CEO of O Type, has worked at Amazon.com-owned Twitch and consulting giant McKinsey. Morrell most recently served as chief financial officer at food delivery company Wonder Group.

Write to Denny Jacob at denny.jacob@wsj.com
